title: Swollen and Hot Testicles in Dogs: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog has swollen, hot testicles, it may be a medical emergency. Learn about orchitis, torsion, and why you should see a vet immediately.
content_html:
Understanding Swollen and Hot Testicles in Dogs

When a dog's testicles become visibly enlarged, firm, or hot to the touch, it is usually a sign of significant inflammation or a vascular issue. This condition is most commonly diagnosed as orchitis (inflammation of the testicle) or epididymitis (inflammation of the sperm-carrying tube). These symptoms can be caused by bacterial infections, trauma, or even autoimmune reactions. Because the area is highly sensitive, these conditions are often extremely painful for your pet.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. You should treat swollen and hot testicles as a medical emergency. While it could be an infection that requires immediate antibiotics, it may also be testicular torsion—a condition where the spermatic cord twists, cutting off the blood supply. Torsion is a critical emergency that can lead to tissue death and systemic shock if not treated surgically within hours. If your dog is lethargic, vomiting, or walking with a stiff gait, seek veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photograph of the scrotal area to your veterinarian or a triage professional can be vital. A photo allows the medical team to see the degree of swelling, the presence of skin discoloration or bruising, and any visible discharge or wounds. This visual information helps the vet determine the severity of the situation and prepare the necessary equipment before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin or ibuprofen for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Human pain relievers like ibuprofen, naproxen, and aspirin can be highly toxic to dogs. Only use pain medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: What if my dog is already neutered but the area is swollen?
faqs.answer: If your dog is neutered, they should not have testicles. Swelling in the scrotal area of a neutered dog may indicate a scrotal hematoma, an abscess, or a tumor, and still requires an immediate veterinary exam.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.answer: In cases of testicular torsion or certain types of trauma and tumors, emergency neutering is the standard treatment to remove the source of pain and prevent further complications.
